cover.title: THE BLADE
cover.subtitle: The one who sees the evidence clearly, then turns the verdict inward.
cover.tagline: You notice what others miss, but too often, you use that precision against yourself.

profile_scores.caption: High stability and warmth, low expression. You see accurately, but apply that standard only to yourself, and stay silent.

short_summary: You do not trust quickly; you watch behavior, compare details, and notice inconsistencies earlier than most people.
Your pain often comes from turning every piece of evidence back on yourself instead of reviewing both sides fairly.
What changes love for you is learning to ask for the real reason before filling the blank with your own blame.

full_report.headline: THE BLADE
full_report.intro: You're not someone who trusts people quickly. You watch behavior, notice what doesn't line up, and see problems early. But instead of speaking that standard out loud, you often turn it inward. This report follows that pattern: why you keep re-examining yourself, why vague people pull you in, and how to review the evidence more fairly.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You're not someone who trusts people quickly.
You watch behavior. Whether what they said yesterday matches what they said today. Whether what they said matches what they did. Whether they're the same now as they were at the start.
So you notice problems pretty early. You see what others miss.
But here's where you take a different path.
Your eye for other people is precise, but you never speak that precise standard out loud.
Instead, you turn it on yourself.
If I'd just paid a little more attention, would it have turned out differently? If I'd done better, would things have changed?
You don't say a word to the other person, but toward yourself, you become the judge who collects evidence most meticulously.
So people think you're generous. But that's not actually true.
The standard didn't disappear. It just all moved over to you.
chapters.profile_note: High stability and warmth, low expression. You see accurately, but apply that standard only to yourself, and stay silent.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Repeat: THE REEXAMINATION CURVE
body: This has probably repeated quite often.
A relationship ends. Or falls apart.
In that moment, you don't blame the other person. Instead, you go back over it.
You replay the conversation. You replay the behavior. You search for where you could have done better.
It's not something you look at once and let go. You look again. And again.
It's like reopening a trial. Reviewing the same case, the same evidence, over and over.
But strangely, that court always reaches the same verdict. You gathered plenty of evidence, but somehow the verdict only ever lists your own mistakes.
Because you only examine your own behavior that closely. You don't look at the other person's behavior nearly as hard.
chapters.highlight: The reason you get hurt isn't that you see too little. It's that everything you saw, you blamed entirely on yourself.

chart.title: THE RE-EXAMINATION CURVE
chart.description: The pattern of repeatedly reviewing only your own behavior, even after the relationship has ended.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.belief: I'm someone who's too sensitive, who nitpicks too much.
items.truth: No. Your standards are high, that's true. But you've almost never held that standard up to someone else. You've only ever held it up to yourself.
items.truth_short: Your standards are high, but you've mostly aimed them at yourself.

items.belief: If I'd done better, it wouldn't have ended.
items.truth: Well. That's possible. But you've never once asked the same question about the other person. The sentence "if they had done better" doesn't exist inside you.
items.truth_short: You ask what you could have done better, but rarely ask what they could have done better.

items.belief: I'm not someone who blames others.
items.truth: True. But the blame didn't disappear. It just moved over to you. The blame still exists, in exactly the same amount. Only the direction changed.
items.truth_short: The blame didn't vanish. It simply changed direction.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: You're drawn to people who don't explain themselves.
That might sound strange. But it's exactly right.
They don't say why they did what they did. Why they were late. Why they went quiet. Why they said it that way.
And you don't just sit there. You start looking for the reason yourself.
They're probably just busy. They're probably just not the expressive type. Something must have happened.
You stay up at night building the explanation that's actually their job to give.
But the explanation you build that way ends up being only your own guess. The real reason still hasn't been said by anyone.
chapters.highlight: Someone who confuses you withholds the reason. Someone who loves you speaks before you have to ask.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't even harsher self-examination. You already do more than enough of that.
What you need is to apply that same scrutiny to the other person, just once.
When there's no explanation, before you make up a reason for them, ask first: What was the real reason this time? Did I guess this, or did I actually hear it?
A guessed-in blank is never as accurate as evidence you actually heard.
Your standard is already accurate enough. Just point it the other way. Before you grade yourself, check whether there's even enough evidence.
chapters.action_sentence: Before you grade yourself, check whether the other person gave you enough evidence in the first place.

chapters.title: Where to Go From Here
body: You know how to refine your own behavior.
You know how to go back over things. You know how to get better.
But what you'll learn next is how to review evidence fairly.
Not every blank space needs to be filled in by your own guess. Explaining is partly the other person's job too.
Once you start applying the same review to both sides equally, relationships become far less lonely.
chapters.transition_note: You've spent a long time learning how to examine yourself. The next report covers why you always look for the cause in yourself, and how you can start sharing that examination fairly.

chapters.title: The Type You Keep Falling For

items.description: You ask how their day went, and they simply smile and say, "Nothing much." The conversation ends there, but your thoughts keep going long after. Whenever a blank space opens up, you end up filling it yourself.
Last month they told it one way. Today the details are different. Late at night, you replay old conversations and quietly compare which version felt true.
Their answers are always soft around the edges. Maybe. I guess. Something like that. The less defined they are, the more completely you build the missing parts with your own guesses.

items.type_title: Someone whose story shifts depending on the situation
items.description: Yesterday's explanation doesn't match today's explanation. So you become the person collecting evidence, piecing together what you heard before and what you're hearing now.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: These signs showed up pretty early. You just filled them all in with your own explanations.

signal_strength.label: You guess at the reason instead of asking, and move on
Even vague things, you sort out yourself and just accept them
The same question keeps coming up, but only your own explanation for it keeps getting more refined
The other person never explains directly. Only you keep analyzing

chapters.core_signal: The same question keeps repeating, but only the explanations you've built keep growing. No one has actually said the real reason yet.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like to You

checklist.title: The other person tells you the reason themselves.
checklist.description: You don't have to make it up for them.

checklist.title: Your standards aren't only harsh on you.
checklist.description: The same scrutiny applies to them too.

checklist.title: Explanations don't only come from one side.
The relationship feels safe even without guessing.
checklist.description: It doesn't fall apart just because you fill in less.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You're weak to people who don't explain themselves. Because they don't tell you the reason, you make one up for them. Since it sounds plausible, they end up talking even less.
sections.who_steadies_you: On the other hand, there's someone who fits you well. Someone who gives the reason before you ask, whose words and actions don't drift apart, who doesn't leave things vague. In front of someone like that, you no longer have to fill the blanks with guesses.
sections.next_signs: This time, you'll notice faster. Right before you start building a reason for them, you'll catch yourself in that exact moment. "Is this something I actually heard, or something I guessed?" That question will come to you sooner than before.
sections.try_this: This time, before reviewing your own behavior, just once, look back at the evidence you've gathered. "Which of these did I actually hear, and which did I make up on my own?" Then review your evidence by the same standard, not just on yourself. If you heard little, that's not because you fell short. It's because the other person didn't say it. The Blade's problem isn't that your standards are too low. It's that the blade has only ever pointed at yourself. This time, point that same blade and examine the other person too, in the same place.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to examine yourself. What's left is learning how to examine the whole relationship fairly.
action_items: Before blaming yourself, separate what you actually heard from what you guessed.
When someone leaves something vague, ask for the reason once instead of filling it in alone.
Apply the same standard to their behavior that you usually apply to your own.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning how to examine yourself. The next report covers why you always look for the cause in yourself, and how you can start sharing that examination fairly.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
